title: Adaptive Tensor Network Sampling for Quantum Optimal Control
authors: Zeki Zeybek, Rick Mukherjee, Peter Schmelcher
abstract: Quantum optimal control (QOC) provides a systematic framework for achieving high-fidelity operations in quantum systems and plays a central role in tasks such as gate synthesis, state transfer, and pulse design. Existing QOC methods broadly fall into two categories: gradient-based and gradient-free algorithms. The associated optimization landscape is often high-dimensional, non-convex, and populated by numerous local minima, making efficient gradient-free search strategies essential. To address this, we introduce a gradient-free matrix product state/tensor train (MPS/TT) sampling heuristic for discrete quantum optimal control. In our approach, the MPS defines a score function over the space of discrete control parameters, which in turn induces a sampling distribution over candidate control sequences. This distribution is iteratively refined through selection of better performing sequences and local tensor updates to bias the search toward high-performing sequences. We evaluate the method on a range of benchmark problems, including single-qubit state transfer, Bell-pair preparation, qutrit gate implementation, and open-system population transfer. Across these tasks, the method exhibits stable convergence behavior and competitive empirical performance relative to established gradient-free baselines. These results suggest that tensor network sampling offers a viable heuristic framework for discrete quantum control.
summary: This paper presents TT-EDA, a new optimization heuristic for quantum optimal control that uses Tensor Trains to represent and sample from control parameter distributions. By using a Matrix Product State to define a score function, the method efficiently searches high-dimensional, non-convex landscapes. The authors demonstrate that the method can successfully recover complex control structures, such as 'bang-bang' pulses and STIRAP protocols, with performance comparable to or better than existing gradient-free algorithms. It is particularly effective at scaling to higher-dimensional control problems.
main_problem: Efficiently navigating high-dimensional, non-convex optimization landscapes in Quantum Optimal Control (QOC) to avoid the curse of dimensionality and the limitations of existing gradient-based and gradient-free methods.
main_result: The proposed TT-EDA method exhibits stable convergence and competitive performance against established gradient-free baselines, showing particular advantages in high-dimensional regimes.
method: A gradient-free sampling heuristic called TT-EDA that uses a Matrix Product State (MPS/Tensor Train) to define an unnormalized score function over discrete control parameters, which is iteratively updated via gradient ascent on the log-scores of elite samples.
model_or_system: The method is tested on various benchmarks including single-qubit state transfer, Bell-pair preparation in coupled spin systems, qutrit gate implementation, and population transfer in three-level open quantum systems governed by the Lindblad master equation.
important_parameters: Bond dimension (chi), number of time steps (L), number of amplitude levels (d), learning rate (eta), and the lambda parameter for controlling the exploration-exploitation tradeoff.
main_assumptions: The method is specifically designed for discrete control parameter spaces and relies on the ability of the Tensor Train to provide a compressed representation of the control space; it also assumes a discretization of continuous control fields.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the TT-EDA optimization loop; Figure 2 illustrates the MPS sampling and update mechanism; Figure 3 and 4 show convergence and pulse profiles for single-qubit tasks; Figure 7 displays convergence and STIRAP-like dynamics for open systems; Figure 8 compares scaling behavior across different dimensions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the TT-EDA algorithm, describes the MPS-based sampling and update mechanism, presents benchmarks across various quantum control tasks (single-qubit, Bell-pair, qutrit, and open systems), evaluates performance against standard optimizers, and analyzes the exploration-exploitation tradeoff using high-dimensional benchmark functions.
why_interesting: It introduces a novel way to use tensor networks not just for simulating many-body states, but as a generative tool for optimizing complex control pulses in open and multi-level quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: The schematic illustration of the optimization loop is shown. (a) Initially, the uniform MPS score func- tion defines a uniform sampling distribution over the dis- crete control space. (b) K candidate solutions are sam- pled from this distribution and evaluated via dynamical simulations to obtain their performance signals. (c) A subset of M high-performing samples is selected as elites, and the MPS score function parameters are updated to increase the scores of these elites, thereby biasing future sampling toward high-performing regions of the search space as shown in (d). This loop of sample, evaluate, fil- ter and update is repeated until the optimization budget is exhausted.

Here the empir- ical distribution is the normalized histogram obtained from samples drawn from the MPS, while the theoret- ical distribution is given by the exact normalized prob- abilities over all bitstrings computed explicitly over the full 24 = 16 configuration space. Rows correspond to the number of arguments (10, 30, 50, 70, 100). Each panel reports the median best objective value as a function of evaluations with interquantile ranges, computed over 10 independent runs.
